title: 2025 11 05 SO
description: Wednesday 5 November 2025 | Standing Orders Committee Hearing of evidence | Review of Standing Orders 2026 Office of the Auditor-General Mark Evans, Director, Sector Engagement Sarah McGray, Principal Policy Advisor, Legal Eleanor Olsen, Senior Advisor Sir Geoffrey Palmer Jonathan Boston Cantin Consulting John Cantin, Cantin Consulting Dave Agnew Anna L Muir Will Dryer Environment and Conservation Organisations of NZ Inc Barry Weeber, Chairperson Cath Wallace, Vice-Chair

chunks.text: Someone like to move the minutes. The last one was, last meeting we held was true and accurate. Record hold.
Why are we hearing from the office, the auditor general? This, this should be short.
Very good.
Very good. Thanks.
Uh, could we call on, uh, Mark Evans, Sarah Gray and Ana Olson?

Right. We're in your hands, please. Uh, mark start. Great. Thank you. Good afternoon. Um, so if you had a short, um, submission from us, we'd just like to, uh, start out by saying that the 2023 standing orders, um, they're highly supportive of. We've had really good, we've got good feedback from, um, those organizations that went through the system and about how much better and more focused the inquiry was around their key bits of operations.

So it's positive thing. We, we've noted that, um, obviously the amount of time to the scrutiny function of parliament is there's dramatically increased. Um, so our submission really focuses on where the implementation of those 23 changes can be further enhanced.

And so you see, we go through a few key areas and we're happy to talk about those with Officer Clark down the track as well in terms of the detail. But scrutiny weeks in particular, you know, we've got one coming up in December, I think at the moment, we're doing about a 95 briefings in that week. Um, and I I, you know, without speaking for committees, we noticed a little bit of scrutiny, fatigue by the, by the end of, of such a week.

Um, and many of you'll remember that before scrutiny weeks, uh, under the previous standing orders, we had departments, um, reviewed by and large before Christmas, some large crown entities, and then the, the rest of the Crown entities done after Christmas. So things were spaced out. So there's a lot of advantages to scrutiny weeks, and we acknowledge that, but as we, we just wonder if the scheduling can be that, uh, a bit more targeted where use of, uh, instruments like defacto, um, proforma, um, briefings could be used for those entities

where there's low risk and the efforts targeted more to those larger, um, uh, entities so that the scheduling can, you know, get the kind of in-depth scrutiny that, that, um, I know everybody's after from those standing orders changes in 23. So, yeah. We've, our brief mentions in there, you know, a couple of suggestions we think could be done around how scrutiny week is scheduled to take, um, to get better scrutiny.

How would you differentiate between, um, um, something that should be more substantially dealt with, something that might be more profile? Yeah, we try to get away from it by, by going to scrutiny. But yeah, I, I think the, uh, the scrutiny plans that, that, uh, committees have, um, already got in place and have implemented with some scrutiny priorities in there.

So they're driven by committees themselves around, historically for these entities, we've been really interested in how they've done on the particular issues that they, or purpose they have. It could be size of entity, just sheer revenue expenditure question, but match to the scrutiny plans. Um, and I think some of the, the small entities that are peripheral to those priorities could be done on a, you know, once a parliamentary term basis rather than squeezed into a, a very hectic schedule and scrutiny week for, for example,

Oh, you, you go first. Oh, You want me to, oh, so yeah, that was gonna be my quick question. Um, 'cause obviously the committees I'm involved in, we, we did, we've actually done a scrutiny plan for the whole three years. Yes. Is it, is it, are you saying that's a bit of inconsistency across select committees doing that?

Or, um, and, and has your office got enough oversight into those or, um, communication around those scrutiny plans or, So we work as, you know, closely with Office of the clerk Yeah. To, to assist and advise. But, you know, ultimately it's the committee's decision to how they want to, you know, conduct scrutiny. But I know the 2023 review did talk about scrutiny over a parliamentary team.

Yes. And I think the idea of that was so that, um, as, as the, the chair was just pointing out that it could be zeroed in around scrutiny priorities, but we've ended up with a bit of a hybrid here where we're trying to do all entities that are annual reviewable in the one week where there's some tools that within those changes that would enable, you know, fewer of them to be done.

Uh, and there are other mechanisms like review briefings that's we've got, yeah. Um, that could be used more. Okay. Yeah. The things seem to be consist issue. Yeah. I've just got two quick questions, Mr. Chair. Um, the first one is, um, in your submission, you noted on, uh, scrutiny week that the practice of having most of annual review and estimates hearing on scrutiny week, um, impacted members' capacity to fully attend.

So my first question was just, um, what was the basis of that? And I guess, have you actually noted that members are not able to attend hearings as a result Of that? I think it was, uh, we didn't, you know, keep a attendance record or anything like that. So, um, I think the main thing we noticed was that by the end there were, um, there were competing priorities that, that the lines and follow up questions were, were fewer.

Um, the engagement had dropped off those kind of really anecdotal type observations. And, and in the end, committees themselves would have to, would be best to test that with. If, if, if that's not the case, then we'd be happy to concede that point. Yeah. And currently standing orders, um, uh, require scrutiny week to be used predominantly for scrutiny, but not exclusively for scrutiny, which means that time could be used for other matters.

Um, do you have a view as to whether scrutiny weeks should be used exclusively and on exclusively for issues of scrutiny to accommodate the use of more time during those days for those issues? Uh, uh, um, not really. I think we'd, we'd say we'd like to see scrutiny done well, uh, carefully around the key performance matters of the entities in question.

You know, in the end, it's up to the, uh, committees themselves to do the scheduling and consultation with, with their colleagues and, and parks. Okay. Um, could I just, uh, move on to the review briefings? We review briefings are a little bit confusing was our observation. Uh, and again, variously used by different committees.

Uh, we found that review briefings, you know, we've still got two to do, um, coming in from last year that are scheduled. Um, for example, they are now, the audit information that is attached to those is, you know, 10 months old. Um, and we felt like it could be clarified in there.

So that's a small procedural, uh, suggestion in there that that could actually connect to what we're saying earlier. There's an opportunity to say, let's do a review briefing for the, the, the term, uh, for this, this particular entity, uh, that we could cover in a, in a, in a summary of audit findings over the last three years or for this particular financial year.

But we just found, like, um, they were not being used systematically or, or perhaps to enable the, the intention of the, uh, original changes. And one last point, Mr. Chairman committee was around the activity activity reports, um, fantastic. Bringing together of, of what was found through the, through the, um, scrutiny term of of year.

Um, I think there's an opportunity maybe for the, in this final year before the, uh, uh, go the next election for the incoming select committees to now pick up the areas of interest to the previous committees in terms of scrutiny priorities and plans and, and what those activity reports might say. We noticed there was varying areas of focus in some of those activities reports, but yeah, I think w we also noticed, uh, regardless of who it is, uh, that the new mps often have to pick up from the start, from scratch.

Um, but yet, you know, of course we're always here, we, we noticed this, what the, the continuing themes you'd say in a, in a financial review of any given entity. And if the previous, the outgoing committee was able to, you know, pass the baton to, to the incoming one would see strength in that. So maybe that be one of the emphasis.

I I think that two issues you raised me, one is the, uh, quality of the, uh, questioning at the end of a, a process. Um, and, uh, then that, that other issue of sort of not even picking up some of the points that, uh, perhaps the auditor's office might, might suggest, should be part of a continuum of scrutiny.

Mm-hmm. And the reason we went to the weak approach was trying to feed some of that. Mm-hmm. Um, but it's not set in stone. It's a, it's a, a work in progress. It might be that we could consider perhaps having scrutiny days as opposed to an entire week that might, uh, help refocus that.

But, um, we obviously, uh, have a lot to do with the auditor's office and, uh, appreciate the work. And I think what I would've observe is that there's, uh, a greater acceptance, not acceptance of, uh, uptake of auditor advice in some of those, uh, scrutiny exercises than that might have been in the past where, you know, whatever was front views or, uh, you know, top of the news was, uh, often what we've got discussed.

But can we thank you very much for your time? Sorry it's a, a brief time, but, uh, we're obvious here in contact your office. I really appreciate it. Thank you very much. No worries. Thank you. Thank you.

Uh, I wonder That Dr. Bot might sit with me 'cause we cooperated in this and followed him, what, half the time. Would it be fantastic the time? No, I don't see always. Thank you very much for making your time available. Us. Well, Mr. Just if you don't mind, Sue, can I just ask, uh, if, um, Joseph Mooney would like to sit at the table?

Storm the end there. Thank you, uh, Mr. Speaker and members of the committee, uh, thank you for hearing, uh, me. And I just note that in my time in the house, I led the creation of the existing select committee system that is now being, or may require a vision.

Uh, and, um, I think that it's interesting that it survived as long as it has, but it does need attention now. Uh, and in that respect, I, I just want to comment to the committee on the submission of the park at the house, which I have read. It's a very extensive submission, uh, by David Wilson.

I submit to you, it's a rich submission and provides ample scope for the committee to make a significant improvement to the operations of the house. I agree strongly that improvement in law making that is an urgent priority because you've just heard about financial scrutiny that has been greatly improved, but the scrutiny of legislation remains to be addressed in a more systematic fashion.

And in the submission I made, I listed about 19 deficiencies that the scrutiny of legislation has in a piece I wrote in about 2010 or something. And none of it has been addressed. So, uh, I I just want to say that I particularly agree with the Clark that efforts should be made to stop the shortening of the legislative process.

Uh, that policy contained in bills needs to be researched and consulted more than it is. And that's con in the Clark's submission and means must be found. I submit to reduce the pressures on the subject matter, select committees over the time the process for those committees has degraded.

And I have found that as a regular submitter to these committees over time, we used to get much more time. We're now lucky to get five minutes or 10. That is not scrutiny, that is going through the motions. I know there are difficult issues in how to fix that. Uh, but there are avenues available as the Clerk's Office has suggested, and what I think is an excellent sufficient, and can I say also that the Clark's work on McGee's fundamental work is a great work, and the Parliament is very lucky to have that parliamentary practice book that the park has produced.

It's a tribute to the New Zealand Parliament, and it's available online for nothing. Uh, the three issues that seem to me to require the most urgent attention from the committee are a curbing on the use of urgency. There are a number of ways that could be done. B not dispensing with select committee scrutiny ever, and improving select committee scrutiny in ways that are, allow the committees to go into more depth.

Now, I know that there is one big reason why it's very difficult to do that. This parliament, the House of Representatives is very small. You take the executive out of it. The number of mps that are available for scrutiny is quite small, and you've got 12 subject select committees to which legislation can go.

And the result is that you have a very uneven workload. And, and I think measures have to be introduced by the standing orders to allow the committees to not hear every piece of evidence, but to hear the ones that the committee thinks can help 'em the most.

In, in, it's a great thing that the democratic process of New Zealand are co uh, causes so many people to make so many submission. Uh, but, but they're overwhelming. You and the mps in this country are terribly overworked compared with internationally because the members of parliament were set in about 1996, and the numbers haven't been increased, but the population has increased.

So you've got my sympathy. It's a much harder life being an MP than it was when I was one. So what I want to say is that the lack, the absence of good technical scrutiny of legislation is really an urgent matter, and you really need to concentrate on that because you'll get better law that endures longer is better considered, and make sure that it has public support behind it.

And neglecting select committee scrutiny is a very unfortunate thing. Now, of course, how Parliament works, you could argue needed a much wider consideration, uh, by an outside body, but that is not something that is appropriate until you've done your best with it.

Uh, and I think that that department can improve its processes and make the legislation better. And I hope something like the report that came out for the financial procedures could come out from this committee about how to improve the legislative processes, because that seems to me to be the most urgent consideration.

And the Clark's submission really reflects that. So I just want to say that these, uh, these matters are of urgent importance for the health of New Zealand's democracy. And this select committee can provide an important contribution to achieving useful change to make New Zealand's democracy more authentic.

Thank you. Well, uh, thank you. Uh, you, you raised some interesting points that I think everyone hears grapple with, particularly this part that is the extent of submissions now being received and the ease with which those submissions can be received, uh, coupled with the expectation that, uh, if you want to be heard, uh, then the expectation should be heard.

And but for MPS to try and sort of defeat that, uh, does require a lot more thought than, than just a, a, a simple committee decision not to hear things. But, um, uh, please ki Karen, thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, sir. Jeff, appreciate that submission very much. Uh, and noting your comments around curtailing the ability for the house to go into urgency.

I'm keen to, uh, go into one particular aspect, which I think is equally important, uh, and worthy of consideration. And that is the ability for a government to move an instruction to a committee, uh, for a shortened report time that doesn't then warrant a debate in the house.

So anything from, as you know, four months in one day, there's no discussion. And, uh, certainly in my relatively short time in Parliament, I've noticed an uptake in that, but also corresponding requests from committees seeking extensions for the time that they need to be able to go through the work that you've identified in your submission.

Now, in a previous standing orders committee report, it said that such behavior should be discouraged, as you know, that means nothing. Yes. Uh, and I'm particularly keen on your thoughts on how we might be able to address that as well. Well, I'm for big constitutional change, not little, and I would change the Constitution Act to put restrictions on the use of urgency.

Uh, and I think that I would also put the same restrictions on a bridging the processes that the standing order set out for the consideration. How is it that a Parliament can decide that the appropriate way to do legislation is to have three readings of committee stage and to do it properly and to have the thing designed in the executive branch before you start and consulted on.

You'd be, it would be a good idea to put exposure drafts out so that they weren't such a mystery to everyone when they finally appear in the house. I won't go into that, but what I'm, but what I'm suggesting to you is that you have the power to make this a lot better than the public, because I think it's a good thing for the public to be able to make the submission.

And as Mr. Speaker is sick, it is invidious for MPS to say you won't be heard. But I think it would be appropriate to set up the Committee of the Clerk and the Auditor General, and some other officials, perhaps the Secretary for Justice, to say that these particular submissions raise important questions the committees should consider for when they're considering the bill.

And all the other submissions that we receive that are not heard will be taken into account. And there will be a report given on them to the select committee by the officials. Uh, and I think that would cover it because it, it seems to me to be a waste of parliamentary time.

They constant repetition at the same points as you often get with, with these select committees. Mm-hmm. Thank you. Uh, so just on that, if, uh, time is is the peak Yes. Pressure always. And I think it was, uh, I think you, you were the, the person who brought in the 30 week year, the sort of Yes.

Spreading it out a bit better. Yeah. Uh, and so three days a week, 17 sitting hours during that time, take out question time, general debate, and you've got something like 13 and a half hours, which is not a long time for Parliament itself to be considered legislation. Do you think there's a case to look at, uh, longer hours and dual activity that can go on the moment?

We don't have Parliament City Walls liquidity set generally, no. Uh, we can do, but not generally. Yes. Well, Mr. Speaker, uh, what I think about that is that the Parliament does not sit as much now in number of hours as it used to. Mm-hmm. And if you compare the number of hours, and there's a, there's a, a, an appendix to my submission that compares how Parliament sits in our, the New Zealand Parliament sits far less in number of hours than the House of Commons in the United Kingdom.

But the amount of legislation is probably very similar. Uh, and, and I think the only way out of this is to have more mps so they can, uh, are not work to death, but can, when they sit on a subject select committee, they're only on one that they can do their work properly and really hold the, uh, government to account.

Because we, we sometimes forget in our, in our unique little democracy, uh, that we have a stripped or racing version of the Westminster model here, uh, we have very little accountability in a whole lot of executive power. And it's all always been like that.

And the upper house was abolished in 1950, and I don't advocate bringing it back, but I do say, and I think this opinion is becoming more prevalent in the community now, that you will get better law if you have more mps and they can concentrate on partic and specialize a bit.

For example, one weakness, the Bill of Rights Act, is one of the interests that I have on previous select committees. I've said, we should have a subject select committee on human rights. You can't do it. And the reason you can't do it is, is you can't have another subject select committee. 'cause there are not enough mps to to, to be there.

And, and that is a really grave weakness in the, in the system where the number of people in the community's gone up from about three and a half million to 5 million in, in the time that the number of mps has been the same. So, so I think there are some bigger issues, and you can't grapple that it's outside the terms reference withstanding yours.

But, but nevertheless, I think it's something you probably need to consider. And, and as, as for urgency, if you have a select a, a process that has been laid down for generations about the number of readings and, and what you are allowed to do, the first thing to say is, we used to sit Friday mornings when I came here.

We don't now. Uh, and, and we also used to sit Thursday nights, uh, which we don't now. And you cannot process in any way that is reasonable. The amount of legislation that is being processed now without All Parliament time to debate it, debate is important.

The time for debate has gone down. We used to get 20 minutes on a second reading, you get 10 minutes. Now. I watched the Parliament when it was debating the, uh, the, uh, recent changes, uh, to the voting and, and the whole debate took an hour. Now, that is not a democratic process, that is a shambles.

And I, I think the Parliament has to consider its own reputation. The parliament has been reduced in public esteem gradually, but surely. And for that to be a risk, the process is adopted here, you have to be authentically democratic, and they're not because of the strains you are under.

I, I'd like to ask you some more questions on that, but Can we go to Ricardo just very briefly? Um, and first of all, I agree with your comments on the Clark submission, but my question was in relationship to the comments you made, um, when it came to oral submissions. Yes. Um, how would you mitigate if select committees were, for example, to hand pick submitters to hear from that we narrow the voices of submitters to people who, for example, have a connection to mps or have a public profile, which may not necessarily equate

to being communities affected by a bill or expertise necessarily. What safeguards would you wanna, would Suggest? I, I don't think the officials should be, I don't think that the committee should be doing it, because you can't, you can't take an MP away from what the MP believes.

But you can get expert advice from a group of senior officials as to which, which missions are likely to be the most use. And parliamentary council should be part of that because parliamentary council draft these bills. And the drafting is often the key thing.

Uh, and, and that, that is one of the reasons why exposure drafts should be set out. So you get the drafting problems set, uh, decided before it ever came into the house. That's that not popular thing to do. But the idea of having a bill dreamed up in the executive branch with total secrecy and put on an unsuspecting house with a, with an introduction, that's not actually a good way to conduct a public debate in a democracy.

Um, Thank you Chair. Um, um, I've, I've heard you talk similarly before about capacity and capacity of MP's number. Mm. Um, and just realizing you've just said that, um, the house used to sit on a Thursday evening and a Friday morning. So I, I, I was initially thinking, well, we can't control time, but we can increase capacity.

What would that number be in your mind, given your experience and your knowledge of the system in which you had designed? Well, Everything depends on how much work the executive wants to give the house. I mean, I remember coming here when the house was closed pretty well, while the milking cows of milking was going on, and the house would come together after an election in about June.

I remember. Uh, and, and, you know, things were, there wasn't as much legislation. There was much less. And the community was a not as complicated as it is now. Some of this legislation take climate change, for example, that's a horrendously complex matter with science and everything in it. How the health, how can the house get that right but only looks at it in a decry fashion?

A lot of issues are like that now. And I've always thought that you could have extended hearings on difficult bills in the legislative council chamber with an extended select committee where, uh, when things are very difficult, when the, when the legislation is a novel b big and very important. And, and I just think those judgments have to be made because, because life is getting terribly difficult for any legislature anyway, democracy around the world is in a state of decay and challenge.

There are fewer democracies around than the were. And if you're not very careful, I just, uh, put a chapter in a book internationally that's coming out very soon about the challenges that democracies that are under. And that is a very unfortunate thing. We're not anywhere near down the bad end of that, but we'll get there if we don't do something.

Um, just on that issue of the use of the legislative chamber effectively, that that room, uh, for, for not necessarily an upper house, but another place that was considered a while back. Yes. So the compromise on that was the extended settings that we Yes.

Now can have. But, uh, they of course, where they were supposed to be for legislation that was broadly supported by the house, it's now just another vehicle. I know. And, and that happens, doesn't it? Yeah. But, um, I, I think, um, the committee should probably have a, another look at all that while we are on this particular, I think you'd only have to do it Mr.

Speaker with, with one or two bills a year. But it would make a big difference on high stuff of great difficulty and public controversy. And the hearings could be, could be televised, and it, and it would be, it would be a super hearing, if you see what I mean. You'd probably have as many people on it as the old statute division committee, which was about 14, I think.

Uh, that was a very interesting committee to be on when I first came to the Parliament was labor lawyers and National Party Farms. And it was amazing how much common ground you can get between. Yeah, I, I'll just make a point that that total time spent here in select committees and in the houses, uh, just over 900 hours a year is, it's not an efficient spread of time.

Do you have any other questions for, do you, Dr. John, uh, Dr. Boss, uh, boss, do you wanna offer any comments? Can I, can I, if, if that's right, Mr. Speaker. So first of all, really want to endorse most of what Jeffrey has said, uh, including his points about the threats to democracy and the fact that we are in the time when there is a declining level of public trust in democratic institutions, including Parliament.

And I think that makes it all the more important that this committee do this particular review very thoroughly and thoughtfully. And I'm, I'm very pleased that you are clearly all engaged. I'd like to underscore what Jeffrey said about the quality of the, um, uh, submission from the, from the Clerk of the House, uh, Dr. David Borson and his staff.

And I think, um, among other things this highlights that this parliament, uh, is served by people of very high caliber, and you should be thankful for that. Uh, the submission obviously raises a number of really serious issues, which this committee, you know, in my view, must address and make some very pertinent proposals for reform.

So, um, David, well done. And to the others, like, um, uh, another David involved. Um, excellent. Just three quick points from me, Mr. Speaker. Uh, the first would be regarding the high volume of public submissions. Uh, here I would probably beg to differ a little bit with, to Jeffrey.

Um, I think there is a case where you have hundreds of thousands of submissions for having a more selective process, in which, as I've set out in my submission, you could have a range of, of possibilities, um, which could be combined. One would be mps on that committee nominating particular submissions for oral presentation.

You could have a random selection process for the rest in which an X number of submitters are given the chance to submit. And you could have what Jeffrey has suggested, uh, uh, uh, uh, a group of people of high caliber, uh, recommending other, uh, submitters be received if they haven't already been included in those two former processes.

So I think there's ways forward, which would avoid some of what we've witnessed in the last year or two with vast numbers of submissions, you folk having to sit through ever, uh, just very large numbers of repetitive, um, presentations with very little chance for discussion and debate. Uh, I think that's a terrible waste of balance time, to be honest, Mr.

Speaker, and I think you have it in your power in this committee to do something about that. There's two other things, just very briefly. It's unlikely we're going to be able to increase the number of mps anytime soon, even though I strongly think, as with the Jeffrey, that would be desirable. But Parliament can increase its advisory capacity, uh, as the, uh, staff from the o office of the Auditor General were highlighting earlier today.

Um, their capacity to contribute to parliament's process through the scrutiny weeks, I think has been in incredibly helpful, uh, for democracy and for the work of mps. But we lack some of the resources that other parliaments around the world have. We, we don't have a parliamentary budget office, for example, and we don't have a parliamentary science advisor, a chief science advisor.

Many departments have chief science advisors. Parliament has nothing in the science area. And there are very few scientists in Parliament. That really worries me actually, because so many of the issues that you folk have to grapple with have a strong science background. And you are often very heavily dependent on, uh, a limited number of, um, advisors, uh, uh, often people working for the executive.

So increasing the, the advisory capacity of Parliament, in my view is, is really important. I appreciate it's beyond the scope of this committee, per se, but it's not beyond your capacity to recommend that something being done about that. And then the final thing just to mention is that, uh, and I mentioned this partly because many of the members are, are on this committee, are are new since, since, um, The, the clerk asked, uh, myself and, and David and others to undertake, uh, a thorough analysis of how Parliament could increase its, um, long-term scrutiny

of the executive, um, in the interest of better foresight, insight, and oversight. And, and I, along with David and others, uh, produced a very large report back in 2019, which was, um, reviewed by Standing Orders in 2020. And I think, so you were a member of the committee at the time. Uh, it, in it, it continues to include a significant number of recommendations, which I think, well, we weren't allowed to call 'em recommendations, proposals, proposals, uh, that are worth, uh, it is your, your consideration.

And so I would simply commend that to you. Um, partly because, uh, uh, protecting long-term interests is a vital, uh, and critical and ongoing responsibility of parliaments here and around the world. Thank you. Sue, Did you have a final question? Um, no, it's okay. Thank you. Uh, well, uh, Lawrence, um, uh, Lawrence, uh, is this a concise question or is it, It It can be a very concise question, which, uh, Sarah Jeffrey Palmer, thank you so much.

I want to check one of the things that David Clark submitted on was the fact that while we push a lot of both through, but we also don't have the same mechanism like in the UK with the upper and lower house. So there is in some ways more executive power. How would you then address what you said before in terms of UK sits more than us, But, well, the UK has two houses, and the House of Ruin has a big influence on the, uh, scrutiny of legislation and sometimes a lot of expertise because there are life peers of the scientists and various,

and law lords and all sorts of things. Uh, and, and they have a lot of people that draw. We have less expertise in this country. It's a small country. Uh, and, and we have a, a lot fewer resources than they do in their civil service, much bigger. Uh, and you can get a lot more outta it.

So, uh, what I, uh, and I spent two weeks when I was a Deputy Lee, the opposition watching the House of Commons. And the interesting thing about the House of Commons is there's not enough room to have a caucus. So they don't caucus. And, and, and what they do is the mps from both parties ask as many difficult questions of the civil servants, regardless of what party there on that is scrutiny that we don't have, we have corpus control.
And that's an anti invention coming here from Australia. And there's a question as to whether it is the best way to proceed. Thank you. It's not,

uh, well, gentlemen, thank you very much. Can I just, that document that you've just, uh, uh, reference from the 2019 Yes. A vague, vague memory of it. So I might ask. It's a door stop really. Well, I've asked about to circulate to members of the committee, so we have a bit of a look at it. Again, thank you. Thank you very, thank you very much.

Thank you. Members committee. And Mr. Speaker, you No haven't. Thank you. It's pretty big. It's fine, but it's with and nosy. I'm alright for now. Full on, uh, Caitlin Consulting, John Pat, Kent, Lin, Kenton. Sorry. My apologies.
